topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical weights pressing down on my chest. I stood by the entrance, my hands twisting the hem of my worn cardigan, watching the crowd mill about the canvas that was mine. It was a vibrant explosion of color, a piece I had poured months of sleepless nights into. Now, it hung there, bathed in the cool, indifferent glow of professional spotlights, surrounded by people who didn't know my struggle, my intent. A tightness gripped my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I took a hesitant step forward, then stopped, the movement feeling heavy, like wading through thick water. A quiet, almost inaudible murmur drifted toward me, and I simply turned my back, focusing intently on the texture of the marble floor, wishing the ground would swallow me whole.

[story 2]

My vision blurred at the edges. Every glance directed toward my painting felt like a tiny, sharp pinprick. I shuffled along the periphery of the room, my shoulders hunched inward as if trying to shrink my frame. The air tasted stale, thick with the scent of expensive perfume and unearned admiration. When a woman stopped to admire the piece, her smile was bright and utterly disconnected, and a wave of profound deflation washed over me. I clutched the strap of my bag so tightly my knuckles turned white. I wanted nothing more than to dissolve into the shadows, to become invisible. I mumbled something incoherent to myself, a low, defeated sound that was instantly swallowed by the ambient noise, and I began walking toward the exit, my pace slow and burdened.

[story 3]

The silence in the room, punctuated only by the polite rustle of expensive fabric, amplified the feeling of being utterly exposed. I watched as a critic circled the work, nodding sagely, their praise landing on the canvas like stones. A strange, hollow ache settled deep in my stomach, a familiar, sickening drop. I wanted to retreat, to disappear into the anonymity of the city streets. My gaze kept flicking back to the painting, a desperate attempt to anchor myself to something real, something tangible, even though it was now claimed by others. I lowered my chin, pressing my lips together until they were white, and slowly, deliberately, I began to walk toward the doorway, each step an act of reluctant surrender.
